A derivation from Proto-Germanic *aik-iþja- (“oak forest”) is also in the realm of possibility...

s.def: a hamlet in De Wolden, Drenthe, Netherlands

eemten.e: First attested as emptun in 1400. Compound of dialectal eem (“watercourse”) and tuin (“garden, enclosed area”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hoogeveen, Drenthe, Netherlands
a village in Midden-Drenthe, Drenthe, Netherlands

elp.e: First attested as in elpe in 1362. Likely derived from an ancient hydronym, which may derive in turn from a compound of a term cognate to Gothic aljan (“diligence, zeal”) and Proto-Germanic *apa (“current, watercourse”). Compare Aalden, Eelde and Elpe.

s.def: a hamlet in Midden-Drenthe, Drenthe, Netherlands

eursing.e: First attested as Ouersche in 1634. Derived from a contracted compound of over (“on the other side of”) and es (“farmland next to a village”) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing-.

s.def: a village in Coevorden, Drenthe, Netherlands

erm.e: First attested as in erme between approximately 1267 and 1290. Etymology unknown. Potentially a prehistoric and pre-Germanic name.

s.def: a hamlet in Coevorden, Drenthe, Netherlands

eldijk.e:
First attested as de Eltdijk in 1851-1855. Compound of the toponym Elten (referring to a section of farmland) and dijk (“levee”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Eldiek.

s.def: a village in Emmen, Drenthe, Netherlands

emmer-compascuum.e:
First attested as Emmer Compascuum in 1867. Compound of the toponym Emmen and Latin compascuum (“communal pasture”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Emmer-Kompas.

s.def: a village in Emmen, Drenthe, Netherlands

emmer-erfscheidenveen.e: First attested as Erfscheiden Veen in 1851-1855. Compound of the toponym Emmen and a compound toponym derived from erfscheider (“person appointed to determine the boundaries of a property”) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”).

s.def: a hamlet in Emmen, Drenthe, Netherlands

ermerveen.e: First attested as Het ErmerVeen in 1851-1855. Compound of the toponym Erm and veen (“peat bog, marsh”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Emmen, Drenthe, Netherlands

emmerschans.e: Attested as EMMERSCHANS in 1843. Compound of the toponym Emmen and schans (“sconce”). Named after a former redoubt. Formerly an independent village.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Emmen, Drenthe, Netherlands

emmermeer.e: Attested as Emmermeer in 1643. Compound of the toponym Emmen and meer (“lake”). Named after a former lake.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Emmen, Drenthe, Netherlands

emmerhout.e: First attested as ghelegen in emmerholt in 1490. Compound of the toponym Emmen and hout (“rising deciduous forest”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Emmen, Drenthe, Netherlands

eerste kruisdiep.e: Derived from eerst (“first”) and kruisdiep (“side canal”). Named after a nearby canal.

s.def: a village in Borger-Odoorn, Drenthe, Netherlands

ees.e:
First attested as de ese in 1263. Derived from a term cognate to West Frisian ies (“farmland around a village”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Ies.

s.def: a village in Borger-Odoorn, Drenthe, Netherlands

eesergroen.e:
Attested as Eesergroen of Eesgroen in 1942. Compound of the toponym Ees and groen (“greenery”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Iesergruun.

s.def: a village in Borger-Odoorn, Drenthe, Netherlands

eeserveen.e:
First attested as Eesterveen in 1851-1855. Compound of the toponym Ees and veen (“peat bog, marsh”).

s.def: a village in Borger-Odoorn, Drenthe, Netherlands

exloo.e:
First attested as tot exle in 1376. Presumably derived from Old Saxon *êkeslô (“oak forest”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Eksel.

s.def: a village in Borger-Odoorn, Drenthe, Netherlands

eerste exloërmond.e:
First attested as Noorder Hoofddiep of Exlooder Mond in 1851-1855. Derived from eerst (“first”) and a compound of the toponym Exloo and mond (“small canal that links up with a primary canal”). Named after a nearby canal.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Eerste Ekselermond.

s.def: a hamlet in Borger-Odoorn, Drenthe, Netherlands

exloërkijl.e: First attested as Exloërkijl in 1913. Compound of the toponym Exloo and kijl (“deeper part of a ditch draining a turf extraction area”).

s.def: a hamlet in Borger-Odoorn, Drenthe, Netherlands

exloërveen.e:
First attested as Exloërveen in 1899. Compound of the toponym Exloo and veen (“peat bog, marsh”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Ekselerveen.

s.def: a hamlet in Borger-Odoorn, Drenthe, Netherlands

ellertshaar.e:
First attested as ELDERSHAAR in 1851. Compound of dialectal elder (“alder, Alnus sp.”) and haar (“sandy ridge”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Ellertshaor.

s.def: a village in Aa en Hunze, Drenthe, Netherlands

eext.e:

eexterveen.e:
First attested as egesteruene in 1479. Compound of the toponym Eext and veen (“peat bog, marsh”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Ieksterveen.

s.def: a village in Aa en Hunze, Drenthe, Netherlands

eexterveenschekanaal.e:
First attested as Eexterveensche kanaal of Eexterveensche Compagnie in 1899. Compound of the toponym Eexterveen and kanaal (“canal”). Named after a nearby canal.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Ieksterveensekenaol.

s.def: a village in Aa en Hunze, Drenthe, Netherlands

eexterzandvoort.e:
First attested as de Zandvoort in 1851-1855. Compound of the toponym Eext, zand (“sand”) and voort (“ford, crossing”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Ieksterzaandvoort.

s.def: a village in Aa en Hunze, Drenthe, Netherlands

ekehaar.e:
First attested as hekehair in 1423. Compound of a dialectal variant of eik (“oak, Quercus sp.”) and haar (“sandy ridge”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Ekehaor.

s.def: a village in Aa en Hunze, Drenthe, Netherlands

eldersloo.e: First attested as heilerslo in 1302. Presumably a compound of Middle Dutch elre (“alder, Alnus sp.”) and lo (“light forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Aa en Hunze, Drenthe, Netherlands

eerste dwarsdiep.e: First attested as Dwarsdiep (Eerste en Tweede) in 1899. Derived from eerst (“first”) and a compound of dwars (“crossways”) and diep (“canal”). Named after a nearby canal.

s.def: a hamlet in Aa en Hunze, Drenthe, Netherlands

eleveld.e: First attested as eleuelden in 1427. Compound of an unclear first element and veld (“field”). The first element has been variously interpreted as Old Saxon *elo (“yellow”) (as a reference to plants growing near the hamlet), Old Saxon Elo (a personal name) and Old Dutch elo (“moose”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Tynaarlo, Drenthe, Netherlands

eelde.e:
First attested as elde in 1139. Etymology uncertain. Unlikely to be derived from Middle Dutch ele (“moose”). One hypothesis proposes a link to eelt (“callous”), here referring to a hard layer of peat. Compare Aalden and Elp.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Eel.

s.def: a village in Tynaarlo, Drenthe, Netherlands

eelderwolde.e: First attested as to eelderwolde in 1442. Compound of the toponym Eelde and wold (“swamp forest, marsh forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noordenveld, Drenthe, Netherlands

een-west.e:
Compound of the toponym Een and west (“west-, western”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Ein-West.

s.def: a hamlet in Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

emmerwolde.e: First attested as in amuthariouualda in the 11th century. Compound of Old Frisian *Amuthwari (“inhabitants of the Emden region”) and wold (“marsh forest, swamp forest”). Compare Hemerwolde and Lutjewolde.

s.def: a hamlet in Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

euvelgunne.e: First attested as bi de Ovelgunne in 1720. Likely derived from Middle Low German Ovelgunne (“domicile of the Devil”), here used in a figurative sense to refer to a piece of land considered haunted or unfortunate. A nearby ruined castle may have inspired the toponym. Compare Gun.

eemsdelta.e: Compound of the hydronym Eems and delta (“delta”).

s.def: a hamlet in Midden-Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands

ellersinghuizen.e:
First attested as tho Ellersynckhuesen in 1515. Compound of Ellersing (a surname) and the plural form of huis (“house”). Compare Ellerhuizen, Jipsinghuizen and Wollinghuizen.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Ellerskhoezen, Ellerskenhoezen.

s.def: a hamlet in Oldambt, Groningen, Netherlands

ekamp.e:
First attested as de Eekamp in 1781. Compound of the hydronym Oude Ee and kamp (“enclosed piece of land”).

eexta.e: First attested as exta in 1391. Etymology uncertain. Potentially derived from an ancient hydronym, derived in turn from Proto-Indo-European *h₂eǵ- (“move”). Compare Eext. Formerly an independent village.

s.def: a village in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

eenum.e: First attested as enun in the 11th century. Etymology unknown. Potentially derived from a prehistoric hydronym or a personal name. Compare Eine.

s.def: a hamlet in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

eekwerd.e:
First attested as equart in 1460. Compound of Old Frisian eek (“oak, Quercus sp.”) and werd (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Aikwerd.

s.def: a hamlet in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

eekwerderdraai.e:
First attested as EEKWERDERTIL in 1851. Compound of the toponym Eekwerd and draai (“swing bridge”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Aikwerderdraai.

s.def: a hamlet in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

enzelens.e: First attested as angeslengi in 944. Derived from either Old Frisian Angisil (a personal name) or Ansgisl (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi-.

eemsmond.e: Compound of the hydronym Eems and mond (“mouth”).

eenrum.e:
First attested as arnarion in the 10th or 11th century. Etymology unknown. The toponym is of prehistoric origin.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Ainrom.

s.def: a village in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

eppenhuizen.e:
First attested as eppinghahusum in 1390. Compound of Old Frisian Eppinga (a surname) and the plural form of huis (“house”). Compare Apenhuizen and Apenheim.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Eppenhoezen.

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

ellerhuizen.e:
First attested as aylardahusum in 1384. Compound of Old Frisian Aylarda (a surname) and the plural form of huis (“house”). Compare Ellersinghuizen.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Ellerhoezen.

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

ernstheem.e:
First attested as ernestahusum and ernstahusun in 1281. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian Ernest (a personal name) and the dative plural form of hūs (“house”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Eernsum.

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

eelswerd.e: First attested as ellasuurð in the 10th or 11th century. Compound of Old Frisian Elli (a personal name) and uurð (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

elens.e: First attested as alingi in the 10th or 11th century. Derived from Old Frisian Alo (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi-.

enumatil.e:
First attested as enematil in 1456. Compound of Old Frisian Enuma (a surname) and til (“fixed bridge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

eekeburen.e: First attested as Eekebuiren in 1781. Compound of Old Frisian ek (“oak, Quercus sp.”) and the plural form of buur (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

eibersburen.e: First attested as Eijbersburen in 1781. Compound of West Frisian Eibert (a personal name) and the plural form of buur (“house, cottage”).

engwierum.e: First attested as edygwerum in 1447. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian Ede (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and wier (“artificial mound used for habitation”). The Dutch version of the toponym is derived from an older form of the Frisian name.

ezumazijl.e: First attested as esmerzijl in 1422. Calque of West Frisian Iesumasyl, derived in turn from a compound of the toponym Eesum and Old Frisian sîl (“discharge sluice”).

s.def: a village in Tytsjerksteradiel, Friesland, Netherlands

eernewoude.e: First attested as eerndwaud in 1471. Borrowed from West Frisian Earnewâld, derived from a compound of an unclear first element and Old Frisian wald (“marsh forest, swamp forest”). The first element may be a personal name.

s.def: a village in Tytsjerksteradiel, Friesland, Netherlands

eestrum.e: First attested as esten in 1419. Borrowed from an earlier form of West Frisian Jistrum, a toponym of unknown origin. Compare Eesterga.

egbertsgaasten.e: First attested as Egberts Gaesten in 1664. Compound of Egbert (a personal name) and the plural form of West Frisian gaast (“elevated sandy land”).

echtenerbrug.e: First attested as Echterbrug in 1718. Compound of the toponym Echten and brug (“bridge”).

elahuizen.e: First attested as elahuysen in 1412. Calque of West Frisian Ealahuzen, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Ele (a personal name) and the plural form of hūs (“house”).

exmorrazijl.e: Calque of West Frisian Eksmoardersyl, a compound of the toponym Eksmoarre and syl (“discharge sluice”).

engelum.e: First attested as enghelum in 1319. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian Engel (a personal name) or *Angla (“Angles, a Germanic people”) and hēm (“home, settlement”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name. Compare Englum.

escamp.e: First attested as estcamp around 1415. Potentially derived from a term related to Middle Dutch etten (“let graze”) and camp (“enclosed piece of land”). Compare Esveld.

elsbroek.e: First attested as hiltetesbroc in 1105-1120. Compound of Old Frisian Hiltet (a personal name) and brōk (“marsh, marshland”). The first element was gradually reinterpreted to els (“alder, Alnus sp.”).

essoville.e: Borrowed from English Essoville. The neighbourhood was constructed to provide housing for employees of the nearby Esso refinery.

emmastad.e: Compound of Emma (a personal name) and stad (“city”).

esperansa.e: Borrowed from Papiamentu Esperansa, derived from esperansa (“hope”).

elsrijk.e: First attested as Braeckrijk in 1681. Named after a former estate. Originally a compound of braak (“breach”) and rijk (“realm”), currently a compound of els (“alder, Alnus sp.”) and rijk. The estate was renamed by one of its owners, Symon Elskamp, as a jocular reference to his surname.

egelshoek.e: Attested as Egels Hoek around 1734. Compound of Egels (a surname) and hoek (“corner”).

erfgooiersbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of Erfgooier (“male inhabitant of the Gooi with special rights”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”).

etersheim.e: Attested as eitersem [read: ettersem] in 1277. Potentially a compound of Old Frisian *Etter (a personal name) and Middle Dutch heem (“home, settlement”).

emmakwartier.e: Compound of the name of Emma of Waldeck and Pyrmont and kwartier (“quarter, neighbourhood”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after female members of the House of Orange.

engewormer.e: Compound of eng (“narrow”) and the toponym Wormer. The name originally referred to a narrow section of the Wormer.

egmond aan den hoef.e: First attested as ekmunde in 1167. Derived from the toponym Egmond (of unknown etymology) with the identifier aan den Hoef (derived from Middle Dutch huue (“piece of land of a certain size”)). The identifier was added to distinguish the settlement from Egmond aan Zee and Egmond-Binnen.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Bergen, North Holland, Netherlands

egmond aan zee.e: First attested as egmunda super mare in 1449. Derived from the toponym Egmond (of unknown etymology) with the identifier aan zee (derived from aan (“on”) and zee (“sea”)). The identifier was added to distinguish the settlement from Egmond aan den Hoef and Egmond-Binnen.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Bergen, North Holland, Netherlands

egmond-binnen.e: First attested as ekmunde in 922. Derived from the toponym Egmond (of unknown etymology) with the identifier binnen (“inland”). The identifier was added to distinguish the settlement from Egmond aan den Hoef and Egmond aan Zee.

s.def: a hamlet in Bergen, North Holland, Netherlands

egmondermeer.e: Compound of the toponym Egmond and meer (“lake”). Named after a polder.

egmond.e: Named after Egmond aan den Hoef, Egmond aan Zee and Egmond-Binnen.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Dijk en Waard, North Holland, Netherlands

edelstenenwijk.e: Compound of the plural form of edelsteen (“gemstone”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after gemstones.

emaus.e: Attested as Emaüs in 1670. Named after the Biblical city. Originally the name of a farmhouse.

eenigenburg.e: First attested as eimhborch [read: einihborch] in 1289. Compound of Middle Dutch enich (“lonely, abandoned”) and borch (“fortified settlement, castle”). The settlement may have formed around a castle built by Floris V of Holland.

s.def: a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

eesterga.e: First attested as Eestergae in 1505. Likely derived from a compound of Old Frisian ees (“farmland next to a village”) or ā (“watercourse, river”) and gâ (“village, village area”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier variant of the Frisian name. Compare Eestrum.

s.def: a village in Bunschoten, Utrecht, Netherlands

eemdijk.e: First attested as Dyk Huyse in the 18th century. Compound of the hydronym Eem and dijk (“levee”).

eemnes-binnen.e: First attested as emnesse in 1269. Derived from a compound of the hydronym Eem and Middle Dutch nesse (“headland, spit”). The element binnen (“within”) refers to the village's location within the area protected by a former levee and was included to distinguish the settlement from Eemnes-Buiten.

s.def: a city in Eemnes, Utrecht, Netherlands

eemnes-buiten.e: First attested as emnesse in 1269. Derived from a compound of the hydronym Eem and Middle Dutch nesse (“headland, spit”). The element buiten (“without”) refers to the village's location outside of the area protected by a former levee and was included to distinguish the settlement from Eemnes-Binnen.

s.def: a city in Baarn, Utrecht, Netherlands

eembrugge.e: First attested as orientale parte ecclesie de ema in 1254. Compound of the toponym Eem and Middle Dutch brucge (“bridge”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Baarn, Utrecht, Netherlands

eemdal.e: Compound of the hydronym Eem and dal (“dale, valley”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amersfoort, Utrecht, Netherlands

emiclaer.e: First attested as emminclar in 1283. Compound of Old Dutch Emmo (a personal name) or Emo suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- and lār (“intensively cultivated forest, forest pasture”). Named after a former homestead.

s.def: a hamlet in Woudenberg, Utrecht, Netherlands

ekris.e: First attested as eecriis in 1233. Potentially a compound of Middle Dutch eike (“oak, Quercus sp.”) and rijs (“brushwood, young branch”).

s.def: a hamlet in Renswoude, Utrecht, Netherlands

emminkhuizen.e: First attested as imminchusen in 1381-1383. Compound of Old Dutch Immo (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- and the plural form of hūs (“house”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Veenendaal, Utrecht, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Vijfheerenlanden, Utrecht, Netherlands

everdingen.e: First attested as euerdinge around 1215. Potentially derived from Old Dutch Evert (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- in the dative plural form. Compare Goilberdingen and Helsdingen.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Utrecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

elinkwijk.e: Compound of Elink (a surname) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). Named after W.H.A. Elink Schuurman, the initiator of the project which created the neighbourhood.

emiel.e: From Latin Aemilius, a Roman family name possibly from aemulus (“rival”). Cognate with French Émile.

s.def: one of two series of Dutch banknotes issued in the 1950s–70s, designed by Eppo Doeve and Ootje Oxenaar, respectively, featuring portraits of prominent characters from Dutch history

Oxenaar bouwde vanaf 1970 voort op de erflatersserie met zijn biljetdebuut Vondel (5 gulden), vervolgens Frans Hals (10), Sweelinck (25), Michiel de Ruyter (100) en Spinoza (1000). Was het geharrewar om een al dan niet mannelijke, vrouwelijke, katholieke, agnostische of prostestantse erflater eindelijk afgehandeld, dan wachtte de verbeelding van de verkoren historische held nog.

G.H.C. van Dijken, eiste niet meer dan vijf maanden met aftrek van de reeds ondergane voorlopige hechtenis; aangezien de verdachte inmiddels al vijf maanden in de cel had gezeten, lag het voor de hand dat de edelgrootachtbare heren (mrs. Dozy, De Waard en Royer) geen moeite hadden met het verzoek van de verdediger tot onmiddellijke invrijheidsstelling.

(...) de Centrale Afdeeling (...) richtte het volgende ‘Rapport nopens het ontwerp van wet op den Chinaschen theehandel,’ 10 December 1817, tot de Staten-Generaal, die, sedert Napoleon hun den alouden titel van Hoogmogenden, Hautes Puissances, niet meer had gegund, nu met ‘Edelmogende Heeren’ werden aangesproken en in den voor de Waalsche provinciën bestemden Franschen tekst met ‘Nobles et puissants Seigneurs’:(...).
